top_requirements.requirement: Automation/scripting ability (toil reduction, infra-as-code, general automation language)
Observability/monitoring tooling (Prometheus, Grafana, Datadog, or generic "observability/monitoring")
Explicit on-call participation named as a job requirement
Networking fundamentals (TCP/IP, DNS, load balancing, service mesh)

top_requirements.approx_frequency: 65/316 (21%) — notably lower than the reliability-doctrine framing would predict; most postings describe the practice ('define reliability targets', 'own availability') without the SRE-book vocabulary
42/316 (13%) — Python dominates far more than Go in this sample, despite both being cited in the existing brief

top_requirements.requirement: Bachelor's degree stated as a requirement
top_requirements.approx_frequency: 45/316 (14%) — a minority explicitly gate on a degree; most substitute "or equivalent experience"

top_responsibilities: Operate and scale a company's production platform end-to-end: build/maintain the Kubernetes, multi-cloud, and deployment infrastructure that other engineering teams' services run on, not just monitor it from outside.
Design and instrument monitoring/alerting/observability systems, then use them to drive down MTTD/MTTR and reduce alert noise over time.
Carry rotating on-call and own live-incident response: triage, mitigate, escalate, and coordinate cross-team during active outages.
Write and review postmortems / post-incident reviews, and turn recurring failure patterns into automation or architectural fixes (toil reduction).
Build and maintain CI/CD pipelines and deployment tooling (Terraform/Terragrunt, Helm, ArgoCD, similar) so releases are safe and repeatable at scale.
Partner with product/feature engineering teams on architecture and resilience patterns (retries, circuit breakers, capacity planning) for services SRE doesn't own the business logic of.

Own specific infrastructure domains (networking/service mesh, database/storage layer, security posture, cost/capacity) as a named area of ownership within a larger SRE/Platform org, especially at senior/staff levels.

A growing subset (roughly 4% of titles, but qualitatively larger in text) explicitly frame the job around reliability for AI/ML/GPU workloads — training and inference infrastructure uptime, not just conventional web services.

comp_signals.range_text: $181,688 - $213,750 (and a second band $191,250 - $225,000 for a different level/location within the same posting)
$148,750 - $361,000 (very wide single band, Senior ML Engineer, DevOps/SRE)

seniority_distribution_notes: Skewed senior: title-based classification of all 316 postings shows ~50% explicitly Senior/Lead, ~25% Staff/Principal/Distinguished, and only ~22% with no seniority qualifier in the title (mostly mid-level, not junior — very few postings use "II/III/IV" leveling or explicit junior/entry/new-grad language, well under 5%). Combined senior-or-above titling is roughly 75% of the sample. This matches the existing brief's framing that most postings request several years of experience, though the brief's mention of dedicated new-grad SRE pipelines (citing Google) is not visible in this sample — none of the 127 companies here show an entry-level/new-grad SRE posting.
notable_divergence_from_existing_brief: Largely confirms the brief (comp bands track SWE ladders, Python-heavy, Kubernetes/Terraform/cloud-native tooling, on-call and incident ownership central, senior-skewed hiring). Two things worth flagging for review: (1) The brief emphasizes Python AND Go roughly equally as "commonly Python, Go" — this sample shows a large skew toward Python (73%) over Go (13%), so the brief's dual emphasis may overstate Go's prevalence in current postings. (2) Explicit SRE-doctrine vocabulary (SLO/SLA/error budget) appears in only ~21% of postings despite being the brief's central "success criteria" framing — most postings describe the same underlying practice (owning availability/reliability targets) in looser language rather than formal SRE Book terminology, suggesting the brief's heavy anchoring to Google/SRE-Book vocabulary is accurate to the discipline's origins but not to how most non-Google job posts are actually worded. Also worth noting as new signal not really covered in the brief: a distinct and clearly growing sub-cluster of postings (OpenAI, Coinbase, Cerebras, Deepgram, Datadog, Life360, Cisco, Fidelity, Algolia, and others) frame the SRE/Production Engineer role specifically around AI/ML training and inference infrastructure reliability rather than general web-service reliability — a flavor of the role the current brief doesn't call out at all.

dimension_evidence.note: Postings consistently frame scope around a defined service/SLO rather than open-ended business problems; a moderate target/weight is consistent with what's described.
54% of postings explicitly name on-call as a requirement and 60% name incident response directly — consistent with a high interrupt-tolerance target.
On-call (54%) and incident response (60%) are among the most frequent explicit requirements in the corpus, supporting the max weight/target as the defining trait.
Postmortem/RCA ownership (28% explicit) plus the pervasive incident-response and MTTD/MTTR language across the corpus supports a high target.
Distributed-systems language (42%) and staff-level postings routinely describing multi-region/multi-cloud architecture ownership support a high but not maximal target.
Postings routinely span cloud, K8s, networking, databases, and CI/CD simultaneously (2-4 tool families per posting), consistent with a moderate breadth target.

Reliability/availability ownership is the throughline of nearly every posting's role summary, even though explicit 'SLO' vocabulary is less common (21%) than the framing implies — the underlying accountability pattern still holds.
Cross-team collaboration with product/dev teams appears often, but external client-facing language is rare in this sample — consistent with a moderate, internally-scoped target.
No postings emphasize teaching/external knowledge-sharing as a role function; low target is consistent with the sample.
No posting frames the job around a client/account portfolio; low target/weight holds.
No named external-relationship structure observed; consistent with a low target.
None of the stated comp ranges in the sample mention on-call stipends or variable/incentive pay tied to incidents; all ranges read as standard base bands, supporting a low target.

Python (73%) and general automation/scripting (92%) are among the most common requirements, supporting a fairly high coding-intensity target below the max (since operational/incident work is also large).
No travel/on-site-embed language observed in the sample; low target holds.

Reliability/availability framing dominates over adversarial/security framing in this general SRE sample (distinct security-titled SRE roles like 'Senior Security Production Engineer' at CoreWeave are the exception, not norm), consistent with a low-moderate target.
No hardware/physical-constraint language observed generally, though a small robotics/autonomy cluster (Hadrian, Figure AI, Waabi, Zoox) exists — not enough volume to move this off a low target.
Only ~2% of titles in the sample are explicitly management-titled (Sr. Manager, Senior Manager), consistent with people-management being a minority path rather than core to the archetype.
No public-facing/speaking requirement observed in the sample; low target holds.

A visible and likely growing sub-cluster (OpenAI, Coinbase, Cerebras, Deepgram, Datadog, Roku, Fidelity, Algolia, Life360, Cisco) explicitly frames SRE work around AI/ML/GPU infrastructure reliability. Still a small minority by title count (~4%), so a target of 1 is defensible today, but this is worth flagging as an emerging pattern that could justify a small bump in a future pass rather than being dismissed as 'not relevant.'
No mobile-platform-specific requirements observed; low target holds.

Database/storage-layer requirements (Postgres, Redis, ClickHouse, Kafka, etc.) appear moderately often (databases 41%, Kafka/messaging 17%), and several roles are scoped specifically to data/storage-layer SRE (MongoDB Storage Layer Services, Fabric team, etc.) — the current low target may undercount how often data-infra fluency shows up as a stated requirement, though it's rarely the role's primary framing.
Cloud (88%), AWS (76%), Kubernetes (74%), Terraform (63%) are among the most frequent requirements in the entire corpus — strongly supports a high target/weight.
